scenario: You are five minutes away from a leadership team meeting. Someone has sent you the agenda — 12 items, no structure, no priorities. Before everyone walks into the room, you need to sort them: what actually needs a decision today, what is just an update, and what should never have been on the agenda in the first place.
prompt: Sort the 12 agenda items into three clean buckets.

items.text: Uploading team photos from last offsite

scoring.tier_1_rule: Full credit (5pts) in either concept or dual_concept bucket
scoring.tier_2_rule: Half credit (2.5pts) for adjacent concept placement
scoring.tier_3_rule: Zero credit for clearly wrong placement

pattern_labels.strong: Your bucket labels were well-chosen — you identified the right underlying categories.
pattern_labels.partial: Your labels were in the right direction but one or two were slightly off from the core concept.
pattern_labels.weak: Your label choices suggest the three categories were not yet fully distinct in your thinking.

pattern_placement.strong: Your item placements were accurate and decisive.
pattern_placement.partial: Most items landed correctly, but a few ended up in adjacent buckets — close, but not quite precise.
pattern_placement.weak: Several items were placed in buckets that did not match their category — a clearer framework helps here.

trap_item_insight.insight: This one trips most people up. An open debate is a decision waiting to happen — either call it now or formally park it. Leaving it as an unresolved debate is what creates the cluttered meetings this exercise is about.

sample_answer.rationale: The test is one question: does the room need to make a call today? If yes, it is a decision. If it is a report-out with no vote required, it is an update. If it does not belong in a leadership meeting at all, it is a parking lot item.
sample_answer.edge_note: Debate over office layout is the one item where parking lot is equally defensible — if the debate has no proposal behind it yet, the right move is to remove it until someone brings a concrete recommendation.

scenario: You are packing for a 2-day client presentation trip.
prompt: Sort the items into three clean buckets.

scoring.tier_1_rule: Full credit in either concept or dual_concept bucket
scoring.tier_2_rule: Half credit for adjacent concept placement
scoring.tier_3_rule: Zero credit for clearly wrong placement

pattern_labels.strong: Your three categories were cleanly distinct — you named them with precision.
pattern_labels.partial: Your labels were broadly right but one was a bit loose — overlapping with another category.
pattern_labels.weak: Two or more of your labels pointed at the same underlying concept, which made clean sorting harder.

pattern_placement.strong: Items landed in the right buckets — your framework was consistent throughout.
pattern_placement.partial: Most placements were correct. The tech items were the trickiest, and that is expected.
pattern_placement.weak: Several items were placed inconsistently — the line between work tools and personal gear needs a clearer rule.

trap_item_insight.insight: Phone, power bank, and headphones all sit at the work/travel boundary — there is no single right bucket. What matters is that you applied a consistent rule across all three.

sample_answer.rationale: The test is function on this specific trip. Laptop, notebook, and pen exist for the client presentation. Passport, wallet, and phone are how you move and pay. Everything else is personal. Tech items that serve both functions are defensible in either bucket.
sample_answer.edge_note: Phone, power bank, and headphones are genuine dual-bucket items — either work or travel is defensible. Consistent placement across all three is what shows structural thinking.

scenario: You have received the annual performance review for a team member. The notes were dumped into one list. Sort them into three clear categories.
prompt: Sort the items into three clean buckets.
tier_1_items: Enroll in leadership training by Q3

items.text: Can be too detail-oriented in senior meetings
Enroll in leadership training by Q3
Shadow a director in stakeholder meetings

scoring.tier_1_rule: Full credit in either concept or dual_concept bucket
scoring.tier_2_rule: Half credit for adjacent concept placement
scoring.tier_3_rule: Zero credit for clearly wrong placement

pattern_labels.strong: You named the three categories cleanly — observation, gap, and action are distinct in your thinking.
pattern_labels.partial: Your labels were close. The trickiest distinction is between a gap and the action that closes it.
pattern_labels.weak: The line between what someone needs to work on and what they need to do next is worth sharpening.

pattern_placement.strong: Strong placements throughout — you separated observations from commitments clearly.
pattern_placement.partial: Most items landed well. The training items sit at the boundary of gap and action — both are defensible.
pattern_placement.weak: A few items that describe what to do ended up in the gap bucket. The distinction is: a gap describes the problem, an action names the solution.

trap_item_insight.item: Enroll in leadership training by Q3
trap_item_insight.insight: This item sits right at the boundary of Areas to develop and Actions to take — it describes both a gap and a specific commitment to close it. Either bucket is defensible. What matters is that you applied a consistent rule: did you sort by what the note describes, or by what needs to happen next?

sample_answer.rationale: The test is: what kind of information is this? Strengths are observable behaviors the person already demonstrates. Development areas are gaps — things not yet present or calibrated. Actions are specific commitments with a clear owner and timeline.
sample_answer.edge_note: Enroll in leadership training and Complete conflict resolution workshop sit at the boundary of develop and action — they name both the gap and the solution. Either bucket is correct as long as the placement is consistent with how you handled the other training-related items.

scenario: These are support tickets from your product team. Sort them by how they should be handled.
prompt: Sort the items into three clean buckets.

items.text: App crashes on login for 30% of users
Data not saving — users losing work
Search bar shows no results for common queries
Feature X behaves differently on iOS vs Android
Font size too small on mobile

scoring.tier_1_rule: Full credit in either concept or dual_concept bucket
scoring.tier_2_rule: Half credit for adjacent concept placement
scoring.tier_3_rule: Zero credit for clearly wrong placement

pattern_labels.strong: You named the triage categories cleanly — urgency, uncertainty, and low priority are clearly distinct.
pattern_labels.partial: Your labels were close. The trickiest call is the line between fix immediately and investigate — both signal urgency.
pattern_labels.weak: The distinction between something that needs immediate action and something that needs more information first is worth sharpening.

pattern_placement.strong: Clean triage — you separated user-blocking issues from ambiguous ones from feature requests consistently.
pattern_placement.partial: Strong on the extremes — critical and backlog were clear. The investigate bucket is where the nuance lives.
pattern_placement.weak: A few critical items ended up in investigate, or backlog items were escalated. The question to ask: is a user blocked right now?

trap_item_insight.insight: Security vulnerabilities feel like they need investigation — you need to understand the scope before you act. But the standard practice is to treat any reported vulnerability as critical until proven otherwise. The cost of under-reacting is too high.

sample_answer.rationale: The test is: is a user blocked right now? If yes, fix immediately. If something is wrong but the scope is unclear, investigate. If it is a preference or enhancement with no current impact, backlog it.
sample_answer.edge_note: Security vulnerability and export missing records are genuine judgment calls — both could be critical or investigative depending on severity. Either placement is correct as long as the reasoning is consistent.

scenario: You are starting a new job on Monday. Sort these tasks by when you should actually tackle them.
prompt: Sort the items into three clean buckets.

items.text: Get laptop and access set up
Form an opinion on team strategy
Learn the unwritten rules of the culture

scoring.tier_1_rule: Full credit in either concept or dual_concept bucket
scoring.tier_2_rule: Half credit for adjacent concept placement
scoring.tier_3_rule: Zero credit for clearly wrong placement

pattern_labels.strong: Clean time-horizon thinking — you separated immediate, short-term, and longer-term clearly.
pattern_labels.partial: Your labels were mostly right. The trickiest call is the line between Day 1 and end of week.
pattern_labels.weak: The three time horizons were not fully distinct in your sorting — some tasks jumped categories.

pattern_placement.strong: Excellent prioritization — you understood what needs to happen first versus what benefits from more context.
pattern_placement.partial: Strong on the month-1 bucket — you correctly held back the strategic tasks. A few Day 1 vs week 1 calls were close.
pattern_placement.weak: A few items that require time and context — like forming opinions or proposing changes — were placed too early. New environments need listening time before judgment.

trap_item_insight.insight: This is the most common mistake new employees make — acting before they have enough context to be credible. Day 1 enthusiasm is valuable. Day 1 recommendations are usually premature. The first month is for listening.

sample_answer.rationale: The test is: do you have enough information to do this well yet? Day 1 tasks are about showing up, orienting, and making a good first impression. Week 1 is about understanding the landscape. Month 1 is about earning the right to have opinions.
sample_answer.edge_note: Read the team handbook and Complete mandatory HR training are both defensible in Day 1 or end of week — the former depends on whether it is assigned reading, the latter on company policy.

exercise_title: Reasons a project is off track
scenario: These are findings from a project post-mortem. Sort them by root cause category.
prompt: Sort the items into three clean buckets.
sorting_instruction: Think about the category each reason belongs to, not who caused it.
tier_1_items: Feedback was vague and not actionable
Sign-off process was never agreed at kickoff

items.text: Stakeholder wasn't looped in early enough
Team didn't flag risks to the manager
Feedback was vague and not actionable
Scope was not defined clearly at the start
Timeline was too aggressive given resources
Sign-off process was never agreed at kickoff
Final deliverable was submitted without a quality review
Final output didn't match the brief
Testing was skipped due to time pressure

scoring.tier_1_rule: Full credit in either concept or dual_concept bucket
scoring.tier_2_rule: Half credit for adjacent concept placement
scoring.tier_3_rule: Zero credit for clearly wrong placement

pattern_labels.strong: You identified the three root cause categories cleanly — people, structure, and output are distinct lenses.
pattern_labels.partial: Your labels were on the right track. The hardest distinction is between a process failure and an execution failure.
pattern_labels.weak: Root cause analysis requires separating the category of failure from who caused it. That distinction is worth practicing.

pattern_placement.strong: Strong root cause thinking — you categorized by the type of failure, not by who was involved.
pattern_placement.partial: Most placements were accurate. The items that sit at the people/process boundary are the toughest calls in any post-mortem.
pattern_placement.weak: A few items were placed based on who was involved rather than what type of failure it represents. Root cause analysis is about the system, not the person.

trap_item_insight.insight: This looks like a people problem — someone was not aligned. But it is equally a process problem — alignment across functions should have been confirmed at kickoff, not discovered mid-project. Both placements are correct.

sample_answer.rationale: The test is: what category of failure does this represent? People failures are about communication, relationships, and alignment. Process failures are about how the work was set up. Execution failures are about how the work was actually delivered.
sample_answer.edge_note: Three items have dual placements: cross-functional priorities (people/process), vague feedback (people/execution), and sign-off process (process/people). All three reflect the reality that root causes rarely sit in a single category.

scenario: Your most important exam is in exactly one week. You have a full list of things you could do to prepare. Not everything on the list actually helps — and some things feel productive but are really just procrastination. Sort them by when they actually make a difference.
prompt: Sort the 12 tasks into three clean buckets.

items.text: Review your notes from the whole semester
Re-read the textbook cover to cover
Watch a full lecture recording you already attended
Watch a YouTube explainer on a topic you are weak on

scoring.tier_1_rule: Full credit in either concept or dual_concept bucket
scoring.tier_2_rule: Half credit for adjacent concept placement
scoring.tier_3_rule: Zero credit for clearly wrong placement

pattern_labels.strong: You named the three categories precisely — active preparation, consolidation, and elimination are distinct.
pattern_labels.partial: Your labels were broadly right. The skip bucket is the trickiest to name — it has to mean something specific, not just leftover.
pattern_labels.weak: The three categories were not fully distinct in your naming — consider what makes each one different from the others.

pattern_placement.strong: Strong prioritization — you separated active learning from consolidation from procrastination cleanly.
pattern_placement.partial: Mostly accurate. The dual-bucket items — sleep, formulas, study group — all have defensible placements in two buckets.
pattern_placement.weak: A few items that feel productive ended up in the wrong bucket. The test is: does this build retrieval ability, or does it create the feeling of studying without the result?

trap_item_insight.item: Re-read the textbook cover to cover
trap_item_insight.insight: This feels like serious studying. It is not. Passive re-reading is one of the least effective preparation techniques — it creates familiarity without building the recall you need in the exam room. Practice papers beat re-reading every time.

sample_answer.rationale: The test is: does this activity build actual retrieval ability, or does it create the feeling of studying without the result? Active recall — practice papers, reviewing notes, filling gaps — builds the mental pathways you need. Consolidation the night before works for things already learned. Everything else is procrastination dressed as preparation.
sample_answer.edge_note: Sleep, formulas, study group, and YouTube explainers all have defensible placements in two buckets. What matters is that you applied a consistent logic: when does this activity produce the most value?

scenario: You and four friends are planning a trip two months from now. Everyone is excited but no one has done anything yet. Some things need to be locked in immediately or you will lose them. Some things require everyone to agree before anyone can move. Some things do not need a decision until you are actually there. Sort them correctly before the trip falls apart.
prompt: Sort the 12 tasks into three clean buckets.
correct_buckets: Figure it out when we get there

figure_it_out: Figure it out when we get there

label_word_bank: Figure it out when we get there
tier_1_items: Who is responsible for booking what

items.text: Deciding which days everyone can go
Who is responsible for booking what

items.correct_bucket: Figure it out when we get there

items.text: Where to eat lunch on Day 2
items.correct_bucket: Figure it out when we get there
Figure it out when we get there

items.text: Which coffee shop to work from
items.correct_bucket: Figure it out when we get there

scoring.tier_1_rule: Full credit in either concept or dual_concept bucket
scoring.tier_2_rule: Half credit for adjacent concept placement
scoring.tier_3_rule: Zero credit for clearly wrong placement

pattern_labels.strong: Clean categories — you separated urgency, alignment, and flexibility clearly.
pattern_labels.partial: Your labels were close. The trickiest call is the line between things that need group agreement and things you can just book.
pattern_labels.weak: The three categories — time-sensitive, needs alignment, low urgency — were not fully distinct in your naming.

pattern_placement.strong: Excellent. You identified what has a hard external deadline, what needs the group first, and what can wait.
pattern_placement.partial: Strong on flights and accommodation — those were clear. The trap items like travel insurance and vaccinations are where the nuance lives.
pattern_placement.weak: A few items with hard deadlines ended up in the group decision or figure-it-out buckets. The test is: does the outside world impose a deadline, or does the group?

trap_item_insight.insight: This feels like a group conversation — but it is actually an individual purchase with a deadline. Each person books their own, and it needs to be done before departure. The external deadline makes it a book-now item, not a group decision.

sample_answer.rationale: The test is: who or what sets the deadline? Some tasks have a deadline imposed by the outside world — seats fill up, vaccines need lead time, insurance must precede the trip. Some require group alignment before anyone can act. Everything else can wait until you have real information on the ground.
sample_answer.edge_note: Airport transfers, which activities to do, and who books what all have defensible placements in two buckets depending on destination and group style. Consistent application of your rule is what the scoring rewards.
